agent_output: {"executive_summary":"A logistics API experiencing a gradual P99 latency increase from 200ms to 1800ms over two weeks, with no deployment trigger and normal database query times, most likely suffers from one or more of: (1) external API degradation in the geocoding, carrier rates, or payment gateway dependencies, (2) connection pool exhaustion or leak causing requests to queue for connections, or (3) unbounded cache growth or memory pressure causing GC pauses. The gradual onset with no errors strongly points to a resource leak or progressive external degradation rather than a code regression. The fact that database queries look normal eliminates the DB as the bottleneck and redirects focus to external API calls, in-process resource management, and network-layer queuing.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Logistics API Service","responsibility":"Handles inbound requests, orchestrates calls to external APIs, returns shipping/logistics responses","boundaries":"Application process boundary — owns request lifecycle, connection pools, caches"},{"name":"Geocoding Service (External)","responsibility":"Resolves addresses to coordinates","boundaries":"Third-party API, accessed over HTTPS"},{"name":"Carrier Rates API (External)","responsibility":"Provides shipping rate quotes from carriers","boundaries":"Third-party API, accessed over HTTPS"},{"name":"Payment Gateway (External)","responsibility":"Processes payment authorization/capture","boundaries":"Third-party API, accessed over HTTPS"},{"name":"Database","responsibility":"Persists logistics data — orders, shipments, etc.","boundaries":"Internal infrastructure, query times reported normal"}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Logistics API Service","to":"Geocoding Service","contract":"HTTP REST — address in, coordinates out","coupling_type":"runtime dependency"},{"from":"Logistics API Service","to":"Carrier Rates API","contract":"HTTP REST — shipment params in, rate quotes out","coupling_type":"runtime dependency"},{"from":"Logistics API Service","to":"Payment Gateway","contract":"HTTP REST — payment details in, authorization out","coupling_type":"runtime dependency"},{"from":"Logistics API Service","to":"Database","contract":"SQL/ORM queries","coupling_type":"runtime dependency"}],"data_flow":"Inbound request → Logistics API orchestrates calls to 1-3 external APIs (geocoding, rates, payment) potentially sequentially → aggregates results → persists to DB → returns response. Total latency = max(external calls if parallel) or sum(external calls if sequential) + DB time + processing overhead.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Hypothesis-Driven Diagnosis","rationale":"Multiple plausible root causes exist; systematic elimination is required before prescribing fixes"},{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker (recommended)","rationale":"External API degradation should be detected and contained rather than propagated to all requests"},{"pattern":"Bulkhead (recommended)","rationale":"Isolating connection pools per external dependency prevents one slow API from starving others"}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Shipping Orchestration","classification":"core","aggregates":["Shipment","Quote","Route"]},{"name":"Payment Processing","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Payment","Transaction"]},{"name":"Address Resolution","classification":"generic","aggregates":["GeocodedAddress"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Geocoding Service","downstream":"Shipping Orchestration","relationship":"Conformist — service consumes external model as-is"},{"upstream":"Carrier Rates API","downstream":"Shipping Orchestration","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer recommended — translate external rate models"},{"upstream":"Payment Gateway","downstream":"Payment Processing","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer recommended"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Investigate external API latency before internal causes","gains":"Highest probability root cause given the symptom profile (gradual degradation, no errors, normal DB)","sacrifices":"May delay finding an internal cause if it happens to be a resource leak","alternatives_considered":["Start with internal profiling (CPU/memory/GC)","Instrument all layers simultaneously"]},{"decision":"Recommend adding per-dependency observability before architectural changes","gains":"Evidence-based remediation rather than speculative refactoring","sacrifices":"Delays the fix by one diagnostic cycle","alternatives_considered":["Immediately add circuit breakers without diagnosis","Increase timeouts to mask the problem"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"External API call strategy (sequential vs parallel)","evolution_path":"If calls are sequential today, parallelizing independent calls (geocoding + rates) could cut P99 significantly even without fixing the root cause"},{"area":"Timeout and retry configuration","evolution_path":"Adding aggressive timeouts with fallback behavior can cap worst-case latency regardless of external API health"},{"area":"Caching layer for geocoding results","evolution_path":"Address-to-coordinate mappings are highly cacheable; adding a cache eliminates repeated external calls for the same addresses"}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Dependency on three external APIs in the critical path","reason":"Any one of three external services degrading directly impacts user-facing latency; this is an inherent coupling that can only be mitigated, not eliminated"},{"area":"Lack of per-dependency latency metrics","reason":"Without granular observability, diagnosis requires ad-hoc investigation every time performance degrades"}],"risks":[{"risk":"HYPOTHESIS 1 (HIGH PROBABILITY): External API degradation — one or more of the three external APIs has gradually increased response times. The geocoding service is the prime suspect because geocoding APIs often degrade under load or rate-limit transparently by throttling rather than rejecting. The carrier rates API is also suspect if request volume or payload complexity has grown. This explains: gradual onset (external service degrading), no errors (requests still succeed), normal DB (bottleneck is elsewhere).","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Add per-dependency latency histograms immediately. Use curl/wget to measure each external API independently. Check if external providers have published any degradation notices. Add timeouts (e.g., 500ms) with circuit breakers."},{"risk":"HYPOTHESIS 2 (HIGH PROBABILITY): Connection pool exhaustion or leak — HTTP client connection pools to external APIs are slowly leaking or not being returned properly. As the pool fills, new requests queue waiting for a connection. This explains: gradual onset (pool fills over days/weeks), no errors (requests eventually get a connection), normal DB (DB has its own pool which is fine).","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Monitor active/idle/waiting connection counts in HTTP client pools. Check for connections not being closed after response (missing close() on response bodies). Look for HttpClient instances being created per-request instead of reused. Check OS-level with netstat/ss for TIME_WAIT or ESTABLISHED socket accumulation."},{"risk":"HYPOTHESIS 3 (MEDIUM PROBABILITY): Unbounded in-memory cache or data structure growth — a cache (e.g., geocoding results, rate quotes) growing without eviction policy, causing increased GC pressure. GC pauses would manifest as latency spikes at P99 without affecting P50 much, and would worsen gradually as heap fills.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Check heap usage trends over the two-week period. Monitor GC pause times and frequency. Look for caches without TTL or max-size configuration. Profile memory with heap dump if JVM, or equivalent for other runtimes."},{"risk":"HYPOTHESIS 4 (MEDIUM PROBABILITY): Sequential external API calls amplifying individual slowdowns — if geocoding, rates, and payment calls are made sequentially, a 500ms increase in any ONE API becomes a 500ms increase in total response time. Three APIs each degrading 500ms = 1500ms total increase, closely matching the observed 1600ms delta.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Trace a sample of requests end-to-end with distributed tracing (or at minimum, log timestamps before/after each external call). Identify which calls are sequential vs parallel. Parallelize independent calls."},{"risk":"HYPOTHESIS 5 (LOW-MEDIUM PROBABILITY): DNS resolution degradation — DNS lookups for external API hostnames taking longer due to DNS cache expiry, resolver issues, or TTL changes. Each request may re-resolve DNS, adding latency.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Check DNS resolution times for external API hostnames. Verify DNS caching is working. Consider pinning resolved IPs or using a local DNS cache."},{"risk":"HYPOTHESIS 6 (LOW PROBABILITY): TLS session resumption failing — if TLS sessions are not being reused, every request to external APIs incurs a full TLS handshake (adds 50-150ms per call). Unlikely to explain the full 1600ms delta alone but could contribute.","severity":"low","mitigation":"Check if HTTP keep-alive is enabled. Verify TLS session caching. Monitor new vs reused connections."},{"risk":"Lack of observability makes future incidents equally expensive to diagnose","severity":"high","mitigation":"Invest in distributed tracing and per-dependency latency dashboards as a permanent fix regardless of root cause"}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 — DIAGNOSE (immediate, 1-2 days): Add lightweight instrumentation to measure wall-clock time for each external API call independently. Log or emit metrics for: (a) time spent waiting for a connection from the pool, (b) time spent in DNS resolution, (c) time spent in TLS handshake, (d) time spent waiting for first byte from external API, (e) time spent reading response body. Check OS-level socket state with `ss -s` or `netstat -ant | awk '{print $6}' | sort | uniq -c`. Check heap/memory trends. Phase 2 — MITIGATE (days 2-3): Based on findings, apply targeted fixes: set aggressive timeouts (500ms per external call), add circuit breakers, fix connection leaks if found, add cache eviction policies if unbounded caches found, parallelize sequential external calls. Phase 3 — HARDEN (week 2): Add permanent per-dependency latency dashboards, configure alerts on P99 thresholds per dependency, implement bulkhead pattern to isolate connection pools per external service.","files_to_create_or_modify":["Add distributed tracing middleware or interceptor around external API calls","Add connection pool monitoring metrics to HTTP client configuration","Add circuit breaker wrapper around each external API client","Review and fix HTTP client lifecycle management (connection closing, pool configuration)","Add timeout configuration for each external API client"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use the Strangler pattern for instrumentation — wrap existing external API calls with a timing/metrics decorator without changing call semantics. Apply Circuit Breaker (Hystrix/Resilience4j pattern) per external dependency. Use Bulkhead to isolate failure domains. Add structured logging with correlation IDs for request tracing.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT increase timeouts to accommodate slow external APIs — this masks the problem and risks thread/connection exhaustion under load","Do NOT add retries without also adding circuit breakers — retries on a degraded service amplify the problem","Do NOT assume the database dashboard is showing the full picture — check if the dashboard is measuring query execution time only (missing connection acquisition time from the app's DB pool)","Do NOT restart the service as a fix — if this is a leak, restarting only resets the clock; the problem will return","Do NOT trust 'no errors' at face value — check if error handling is swallowing timeouts or treating slow responses as success"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["The root cause is NOT the database — dashboard metrics plus the gradual onset pattern rule this out","The root cause involves external API calls or the infrastructure around them (connection pools, DNS, HTTP client)","Adding per-dependency latency instrumentation is the correct first step regardless of which specific hypothesis is correct","Hypotheses 1 and 2 (external API degradation and connection pool exhaustion) collectively cover ~70% probability"],"low_confidence_areas":["Cannot determine which specific external API is the culprit without instrumentation data","Cannot distinguish between connection pool leak vs external API degradation without socket-level metrics","The GC/memory hypothesis is plausible but would typically show more variance in latency patterns than described"],"assumptions":["The database dashboard is accurately reflecting query execution times (not just slow-query logs with a high threshold)","The 10,000 requests/day volume has been relatively stable (not itself growing, which would change the diagnosis)","No infrastructure changes (network, DNS, load balancer, TLS certificates) occurred during the two-week window","The three external API calls happen in the request hot path, not asynchronously"]}}
